does: This paper identifies and addresses an asymmetry in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) where the loss function suppresses dispreferred responses much faster than it promotes preferred ones, causing models to learn to avoid bad answers rather than generate good ones. The authors propose AdaDPO, a self-adaptive variant that introduces per-preference-pair, stop-gradient-based coefficients derived from the policy model's generation probabilities to enforce equal gradient magnitudes between preferred and dispreferred response components. The method maintains DPO's original hyperparameter structure while adding numerical stability through per-token gradient balancing and clipping bounds. Experiments on Llama-3-8B-Instruct trained on UltraFeedback demonstrate that AdaDPO consistently outperforms standard DPO on AlpacaEval 2 benchmarks.
connects: This work builds directly on recent theoretical analyses of DPO that revealed gradient asymmetry problems in the original formulation, extending the line of research seeking to improve preference-based alignment beyond reward model training and RL loops. DPO itself emerged as a simpler alternative to RLHF, and AdaDPO responds to fundamental limitations discovered in DPO's loss function through more sophisticated gradient manipulation. The approach connects to broader efforts in preference learning and alignment that aim to better balance exploration and exploitation—or in this case, learning to avoid negatives versus learning to promote positives. The reliance on the policy model's own generation probabilities relates to self-normalized importance weighting and adaptive learning rate schemes common in modern optimization.
enables: AdaDPO opens pathways toward more balanced and efficient preference optimization methods that could improve model alignment across diverse domains. The self-adaptive coefficient approach provides a foundation for further refinements in gradient-based preference learning, potentially inspiring similar balancing techniques in other alignment algorithms. By demonstrating that simple architectural adjustments can yield consistent improvements over DPO, this work suggests that other asymmetries or inefficiencies in preference optimization may be addressable through similar gradient analysis. Future work could explore whether the balancing principle generalizes to other loss functions, scales to larger models, or combines with other recent alignment improvements like SimPO, and could investigate whether this better gradient balance translates to improvements in safety properties or robustness to distribution shifts.
